<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: coil-attention-cyclic-memory-and-recurrence-contracts
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/coil-attention-cyclic-memory-and-recurrence-contracts.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/coil-attention-cyclic-memory-and-recurrence-contracts.qmd@957755b9c
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Coil Attention, Cyclic Memory, and Recurrence Contracts

Memory is where structural language most easily starts pretending to be
understanding.

A slot can be fresh without being useful. A lag can be covered without the
right dependency being captured. A recurrence loop can keep producing work
after it has stopped making progress. A ring buffer can look orderly while
hiding alias collisions. Long context can sound continuous while the actual
memory contract is broken.

Coil attention and cyclic memory are valuable only if they make those facts
more visible. The point is not to claim that cyclic structure creates better
retrieval or better reasoning. The point is to give later consumers a record:
which slots were used, how many times an address wrapped, which lags were
covered, what freshness policy applied, when recurrence had to stop, what
fallback existed, and which claims remain blocked.

That is why this chapter treats cyclic memory as a contract layer. It lets
memory become inspectable before it becomes trusted.

## Problem

Long-context systems often fail quietly.

The model may produce fluent text while reading stale state. A sparse attention
pattern may cover the positions it promised while missing the dependency the
task needed. A recurrence schedule may reduce active tokens while creating a
loop that never exits. A residue-only address may hide the fact that multiple
historical items share the same slot. A compressed or cyclic memory may appear
efficient while silently widening the authority of context that was supposed
to remain scoped.

The failure is not cyclic structure itself. Cyclic structure can be useful. It
can expose aliasing, coverage, freshness, recurrence depth, and fallback
behavior in ways that ordinary model traces do not. The failure is claim
confusion: treating those exposed structural facts as evidence that the system
retrieved the right content, reasoned well, saved memory in a useful way, or
extended context length in a task-relevant way.

The stack needs a place for the cheap facts and the hard claims to stay
separate.

## Why Existing Approaches Are Insufficient

Ordinary memory language is too forgiving. A system can say it "remembered" a
document when all it actually preserved was a pointer, slot, key, summary, or
compressed trace. It can say a lag was "covered" when the coverage is
structural rather than semantic. It can say a cache is "fresh" when freshness
only means version recency, not adequacy for the current claim.

VCM and Verification Bandwidth make the distinction sharper. Context
admission, authority, adequacy, freshness, verification capacity, and task
success are not the same property. A governed context object arrives with
address, version, authority, lease, taint, and adequacy state. A memory module
then represents that object inside slots, windows, recurrence schedules, or
compressed traces. The representation may preserve useful structural facts.
It does not, by itself, prove that the model used the information correctly.

The external recurrence and long-range sequence literature gives useful
comparison vocabulary through Transformer-XL, Compressive Transformers, and
RetNet. Those sources help position segment-level recurrence, compressed
memory, retention, and attention/recurrence tradeoffs as known families of
sequence-memory design. In this repository, they remain source-noted
comparators only. Their reported results are not reproduced here, and they do
not make a Coil retrieval-quality, reasoning-quality, speed, memory-savings,
or long-context claim true.

Finite proofs and learned behavior meet at the hardest boundary. A proof can
certify a slot property, coverage condition, alias rule, or loop-exit
condition. It cannot certify that a learned model retrieved the right
information or solved the task unless a workload-specific evaluation supplies
that evidence.

## Core Claim

Coil attention and memory contracts should be used as structural guardrails for
cyclic memory, sparse coverage, recurrence schedules, and work-budget
admission, not as claims of retrieval or reasoning quality.

That claim remains a design rationale at `argument` support. The live chapter,
Appendix C, source notes, external comparator notes, schema fixture, and Lean
hooks describe a structural contract boundary. They do not prove retrieval
quality, reasoning quality, long-context usefulness, speed, memory reduction,
KV-cache behavior, sparse-attention adequacy, learned-memory behavior, or
transfer success.

The useful move is custody, not promotion. A memory contract should say what
happened to the context object as it moved through slots, winding, freshness,
coverage, recurrence, and fallback. It should also say which later claims are
not allowed to ride along.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is the Cyclic Memory Contract.

The contract starts with a memory or attention request and records how the
request becomes a finite structure. It keeps residue and winding together so
slot reuse does not erase history. It records provenance for reused slots. It
keeps VCM packet references attached so context authority and adequacy do not
disappear inside a model component. It records freshness windows, stale-read
policy, sparse coverage, uncovered lags, recurrence schedules, active work,
loop exits, fallback attention, admission state, baselines, probes, residuals,
and non-claims.

The contract is not a quality certificate. It is a guardrail. It tells a
planner whether a memory representation is structurally admissible. It tells a
verifier whether recurrence had an exit. It tells a benchmark adapter what
ordinary baselines must be compared. It tells a downstream route whether a
claim is structural-only, eligible for workload evaluation, blocked, or forced
to fall back.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Governed context packet"] --> B["Slot and residue mapping"]
  B --> C["Winding and provenance"]
  C --> D["Freshness and coverage checks"]
  D --> E["Recurrence budget and exit"]
  E --> F{"Contract admissible?"}
  F -- "yes" --> G["Structural guardrail"]
  F -- "no" --> H["Fallback or residual escrow"]
  G --> I["Consumer may use only supported axes"]
  H --> I
  I --> J["Workload evidence still required for quality"]
```

**What the contract shows:** a context packet can be mapped into cyclic memory
without losing slot, winding, freshness, coverage, recurrence, fallback, and
authority information. Passing that contract admits a structural guardrail. It
does not prove useful memory.

The default response to missing information is conservative. Missing winding
becomes visible alias risk. Partial coverage keeps fallback attention
available. A stale read fails closed or enters residual escrow. A recurrence
budget exits instead of treating more loops as free intelligence. A missing
baseline blocks promotion.

Those are modest controls, but they are the controls that make the idea
testable.

## Interfaces

The Cyclic Memory Contract is the interface between governed context and memory
mechanisms.

At minimum, it needs a contract identity, memory target, memory-authority
scope, VCM packet references, slot count, residue policy, winding policy,
state-carry boundary, freshness window, stale-read policy, coverage claim,
recurrence schedule, loop-exit condition, fallback attention, admission state,
baseline references, probe requirements, authority non-widening rule,
residuals, evidence references, and non-claims.

Different consumers read different parts of the same record. A memory module
uses slot and winding fields. A recurrence controller uses work budget and exit
conditions. A VCM layer checks that authority, lease, taint, and adequacy did
not widen. A verifier checks whether structural facts are being overread. A
benchmark harness checks whether the structural protections improve a workload
against ordinary attention, FIFO, LRU, content-gated memory, slot-only memory,
or slot-plus-winding baselines.

The consumer rule is narrow: no consumer may convert structural coverage into
semantic adequacy without separate evidence. A fresh slot can help decide
whether a route is allowed to proceed. It cannot prove the route knows what it
is doing.

## Invariants

Residue-only aliasing must remain visible. If two historical states can land
in the same residue class, the record must preserve winding or provenance
instead of pretending the residue is enough.

Structural coverage is not semantic coverage. A lag, stride, or sparse orbit
can be covered while the task still lacks the relevant information.

Recurrence budgets require exits. A loop that cannot stop is not a reasoning
advance; it is unbounded work with a story attached.

Stale reads fail closed or enter residual escrow. Old state cannot become
fresh authority just because the model can still speak fluently.

Representation cannot widen authority. Mapping a governed context packet into
slots, recurrence traces, or compressed attention cannot expand its permitted
use, lease, adequacy, or access boundary.

Those invariants are deliberately structural. They make memory safer to test.
They do not claim the memory is useful yet.

## Failure Modes

Alias hiding is the first failure. The system uses residue-only diagnostics and
forgets that multiple histories may share the same apparent address.

Freshness laundering is the second. A current slot or version is treated as if
it proved the content was task-relevant.

Coverage laundering is the third. A sparse pattern covers a structural lag,
then the result is described as if arbitrary semantic dependency were solved.

Overthinking is the recurrence failure. The loop continues because it can,
not because it has a bounded reason to continue.

Baseline omission is the evaluation failure. A cyclic memory mechanism looks
promising because it was never compared against ordinary attention, FIFO, LRU,
content-gated memory, local/global attention, or another simple control.

Each failure produces or preserves a visible state: blocked promotion,
fallback, residual escrow, workload-evaluation-only status, or retirement.
Hidden memory failure is more dangerous than recorded memory failure.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a contract-backed memory and attention
evaluation plan.

The repository already has a valid cyclic-memory contract fixture. It records
memory target, memory-authority scope, VCM packet references, slot count,
residue and winding policies, state-carry boundary, freshness window,
stale-read policy, coverage claim, recurrence schedule, loop exit, fallback,
admission state, baseline references, probe requirements, authority
non-widening, residuals, non-claims, and evidence references.

That fixture validates record shape. It is not a KV-cache freshness checker,
sparse-coverage harness, recurrence benchmark, learned-memory workload,
Circle-generated contract pack, or Theseus transfer consumer. It does not show
retrieval quality, reasoning quality, speed, memory savings, or long-context
utility.

The next honest minimum is a small trace set: one slot reuse with winding
preserved, one stale read blocked or residualized, one sparse coverage gap
that triggers fallback, and one recurrence schedule that exits. Only after
those structural traces exist should a workload harness ask whether the
contracts help task behavior.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a memory-admission and recurrence-control plane.

In that plane, cyclic memory does not sell itself as intelligence. It exposes
the state that later components need in order to decide whether memory is safe
to use. Slot, winding, freshness, aliasing, sparse coverage, active work, loop
exit, fallback, baseline, and residual fields become ordinary admission facts.

The plane would let VCM provide governed context identity and authority, memory
modules provide structural diagnostics, proof receipts preserve finite facts,
and benchmark adapters decide whether those facts improve actual workloads. A
route could use a cyclic memory mechanism as a specialist only when the record
shows that the relevant axis is supported and the unsupported axes remain
blocked.

At maturity, a cyclic memory path would be reversible and narrow. Missing
winding would create alias residuals. Stale reads would fail closed. Coverage
gaps would trigger fallback. Exhausted recurrence budgets would exit. Favorable
workload results would be scoped to the tested workload. Negative controls
would remain attached. Retrieval quality and reasoning quality would move only
through evidence, not through structural elegance.

This remains beyond the current evidence state. The chapter should stay at
`argument` until alias-boundary tests, stale-read probes, sparse-coverage
cases, recurrence-exit traces, retrieval-quality records, workload baselines,
reset behavior, and promotion reviews show when cyclic memory improves
continuity without letting old state become hidden authority.

## Proof And Test Boundary

The Lean hooks for this chapter live in
`AsiStackProofs.CoilAttentionMemory`. They prove narrow finite-record gates:
cyclic memory claims must record residue and winding or visible alias residual
risk, and sparse coverage or freshness facts alone cannot promote a retrieval
quality claim.

Those hooks do not prove KV-cache behavior, sparse-attention coverage,
recurrence-exit behavior, freshness performance, retrieval quality, reasoning
quality, memory savings, speed, long-context usefulness, or model behavior.

The implemented protocol fixture validates the contract shape. The planned
tests still need concrete memory traces, stale-read cases, sparse-coverage
cases, recurrence schedules, baselines, commands, environment notes, and result
artifacts before they can support stronger claims.

## Source Boundary

The chapter uses Coil Attention and Memory for cyclic slots, winding,
freshness, sparse coverage, recurrence budgets, loop exits, work budgets, and
alias diagnostics. It uses the Circle AI Contract Suite for theorem-linked
contract family language. It uses Theseus Circle Transfer for transfer
discipline and public/private result boundaries. It uses VCM for governed
context identity, authority, adequacy, taint, revocation, invalidation, audit,
and non-success states.

Transformer-XL, Compressive Transformers, and RetNet provide external
sequence-memory and recurrence comparators. They are positioning sources here,
not reproduced results.

None of these mappings promotes a Coil memory mechanism to a deployed memory
system, long-context result, retrieval-quality result, reasoning-quality
result, speed result, memory-savings result, or transfer result.

## Summary

Cyclic memory is useful when it makes memory easier to inspect.

A contract can say which slot was used, whether winding was preserved, which
lags were covered, whether a stale read was blocked, when recurrence had to
exit, what fallback remained, and which claims are still non-claims. That is
valuable because it narrows the system's authority over its own memory story.

The architecture should welcome cyclic memory only under that discipline. The
cheap structural facts should be easy to carry. The hard quality claims should
stay hard.

## Handoff

Memory contracts preserve structural facts at the memory boundary. The next
substrate problem is what happens when cyclic structure moves into adapters,
position encodings, route heads, and mixers. CoilRA, MultiCoil RoPE, and Cyclic
Mixers carries the same discipline into those mechanisms: exact structure,
ordinary baselines, hardware costs, workload fit, and failure cases before
adoption.
